chapters.title: Look at your business with fresh eyes
chapters.text:
Modern businesses and marketing campaigns should chase a useful kind of virality. You can set a strong example in a few ways. Find a fresh way to reach your audience, the way Steve Jobs did. Understand the real wishes of your customers, the way social worker Koreen Johannessen does. Surprise your buyers, the way Blendtec's Tom Dickson or journalist Denise Grady does. Pull people in with a one of a kind experience, the way the owners of the Please Don't Tell bar do.

Very few things go viral by accident. Most of the time, virality is the result of a careful plan that takes care of current buyers and pulls in new ones.

The recipe is not hidden. People will pay attention to your services and pass them along when the products make them feel better. If your work stirs up good feelings and brings something new and useful, the spread grows fast. If you can also place your brand's story inside the wider story of human life and earn social proof, you will hold the keys to virality.

Study your audience, watch what your partners build, and try new things without fear. Most of all, enjoy your work. That energy will pass on to your customers.

A quick exercise. Put your idea to the test and lay out a plan to spread it. How many people know about it today. How will it make their lives better. What feelings does your product bring, and what story does it tell. Why would people want to tell others about it.

chapters.title: Mystery pulls in fans of rare finds
chapters.text:
Most people have a few favorite places to meet friends. They know where to grab a healthy breakfast, where to find the best pizza, and where the beer is cheapest. On one hand, these spots save time and cut down on choices. On the other hand, the same cafes can grow stale, and people start craving something new.

Has that ever happened to you. Picture this. You and your friends are talking about a new place, and a stranger at the next table tells you about a secret bar. You will not find it on a map, but they will lead the way. First, you step into an old phone booth and dial a special code.

Why should you care. A new bar or restaurant opens somewhere every week, and most of them push hard with bright ads, glowing reviews, and the widest reach. New restaurateurs will do whatever it takes to please visitors. That is not always what buyers want.

In a world where almost anything is easy to find, some people value being different even more. They chase the chance to see, buy, and own something that others have not heard of.

Getting a peek at rare products, places, and events lifts a person's confidence and standing.

By the way, the bar from the start of the chapter is real. It is called Please Don't Tell, and it sits in New York. It is hard to find unless you know the regulars or the owners. Please Don't Tell is not trying to win over the whole city. There would not be room for everyone, and the bar prizes its air of mystery. That is why it has the highest social currency.

Hopefully, this story shows you when it might make sense to keep a few updates quiet so that your audience wants to know more. Sometimes, less really is more.

chapters.title: Marketing at a sprint or a marathon
chapters.text:
What do people talk about more, their adventures and wild moments, or the everyday stuff of life. You are right if you said it depends on how boring their day was. Who wants to chat about favorite oatmeal or toothpaste when they can share travel stories and discoveries.

Still, a study by Jonah Berger's team found that people are more likely to talk about everyday topics, just because most days are ordinary. There is nothing wrong with that. In fact, the finding opens the door to more research, especially for ad campaigns.

Why. Because many brands chase attention. Of course, the Please Don't Tell bar does the opposite and works hard to stay secret. Yet countless companies dream of landing on personal recommendation lists, slipping into friendly chats, and catching a wave of word of mouth at any cost.

Firms that lean on word of mouth stand out from those chasing the usual ad game.

At the same time, it helps to know that different services need different speeds of spread. For a new restaurant, reaching a lot of people fast is critical, or it will struggle against the established spots.

On the other hand, fast reach is not always the point for elections or social campaigns. Businesses or individuals can still gain when their ideas sit in people's minds longer, so the audience can make the right call at the right time. Even when people disagree or push back against the idea, the idea keeps its grip on their attention.

To go viral, skip the loud slogans and pick the right triggers that spark a reaction. Watch the places where people live, listen to what they talk about and use, and make your services interesting enough that they have no reason to say no.

chapters.title: Use ordinary topics to spark real interest
chapters.text:
In 2008, Denise Grady's article about coughing landed on The New York Times Most E Mailed list. She did not write about cats, cartoons, or cruises. She wrote about a cough. And thousands of people forwarded it to family, friends, and coworkers in one go. Let us look at why.

Save articles, notes, messages, and books that make you think. Come back to them when you need a fresh spark or a new idea.

In the first phase of research on articles that end up on the Most E Mailed list, Jonah Berger's team found that health and education topics were the most popular. That alone did not explain Grady's success.

The next phase built the numbers, and they showed that useful and interesting pieces had a 30 percent edge that helped them land on the list. That part made sense and was probably obvious to the author. Why else write a paper about a cough with no value.

The research took a fresh turn when Berger and his team looked closely at the feelings of readers. They found that people love to learn secrets and life hacks from writers, and that surprise and delight made them want to share. The chance to be the source of those cool facts pushed people to forward the articles to their circle.

The secret of Grady's piece was that it showed a familiar act, coughing, from a fresh angle. People were so taken with the new facts that they did not want to be the only ones in the know.

When we care, we share, as Jonah Berger has written.

Strong negative feelings can also pull people together and hold their attention. To pull this off, you have to highlight the right angles and lay out your point of view clearly. Pieces that stir sadness tend to slip by almost unnoticed.

So, put good energy into your messages. Stir up a sense of wonder, and people will talk about you.

A side note. Albert Einstein, the Nobel Prize winner in physics, saw the bridge between art and science as his deepest source of inspiration and wonder. He believed that anyone could find reasons to marvel, even in the dullest or hardest subject.

chapters.title: People copy the ones they admire
chapters.text:
Next time you feel like you are wasting your time, remember that Steve Jobs and his partner Ken Segall spent long hours on the placement of the Apple logo on their machines.

The small talk made sense because they were weighing another driver of virality, public visibility. They had to pick between two priorities. Serving the current customer or pulling in a new one. Depending on the choice, the Apple logo on the laptop would either look at the user when they opened the lid, or it would face outward toward the people around them. Jobs and Segall went with the second option.

In public, people watch, judge, and compare. They notice what strangers wear, buy, eat, and talk about. If they like what they see, they quietly copy it and think about what they need to match those around them.

That is why fashion designers, restaurateurs, directors, writers, and trainers all want to land on recommendation lists. When most people agree on the quality and value of a service, it becomes a must have for everyone else.

Social proof runs on the fact that people watch what others do and feel good about before making similar choices.

The pull of public behavior on private habits matters not only for businesses but also for social campaigns. The work of clinical social worker Koreen Johannessen at the University of Arizona shows this in action.

While studying youth drinking and looking for ways to cut it, she found that flyers and stats about the harm from alcohol did not move the students at all. Private talks with students pointed to the real issue. Most of them did not like getting drunk, but the topic of parties and binges came up so often in friend groups that they thought everyone was drinking. That insight let Johannessen strip alcohol of its place as the top tool for socializing.

Behavior is public and thoughts are private, as Jonah Berger has written.

chapters.title: Show the practical value of what you offer
chapters.text:
People are always looking for ways to be more productive, save time or money, sharpen their skills, or boost their well being. The strongest pull, though, comes when a product or service makes them look smart in front of friends and family for recommending it.

By sharing high quality, useful information, you can become a go to resource and raise the odds of your ideas spreading.

So another solid route to virality is to spell out the practical value of what you offer. Your buyers will happily turn into ambassadors when you do a few things. Pay attention to their interests, wishes, doubts, and fears. People love when a brand remembers them, cares about them, and steps in to help. In other words, they want the gift list of fresh ideas in November, not in February. Make sure your insights and advice stand out in a sea of other information. After they use it and feel the win, they will feel obliged to pass it on. People help each other more when they are going through the same things. Make your audience feel special and valued. Personal offers, invites to events, and themed discounts help.

Speaking of discounts, which works better, naming the amount of money saved or the percentage. After a string of market studies, experts laid out the 100 rule. If a product costs less than one hundred dollars, show the discount as a percentage. When it costs more, it pays to name the dollar amount saved. A 10 percent discount on a fifty dollar pullover reads better than a sign that says you save five dollars. The same 10 percent off a five thousand dollar music system is less tempting than a sign that flashes five hundred dollars off.

Show your audience the hidden value of your services, and they will stay loyal for a long time.

chapters.title: Open up your customers through stories
chapters.text:
Stories have been part of human life for thousands of years. Our ancestors drew successful hunts on cave walls. As language and music grew, they shared stories by the fire and passed them on in song, handing down lessons and wisdom.

Writing lifted storytelling to a new level. People could now write things down, save them for years, and carry them anywhere to share. The arrival of theater lifted the status and reach of stories even more. Actors playing roles made audiences live, cheer, and worry about the lives of people they had never met.

Human love for stories runs older than books, films, television, the internet, and, of course, seminars on how to tell a good story. That is why people from different cultures can find common ground by listening to and sharing stories that entertain and teach.

Putting a business idea into practice has a lot in common with game design. Every customer wants to stand out from the other players, earn extra points, and feel like a winner.

Ads have become the shortest and most popular stories of our time. With only a few seconds to work with, creative teams try to make viewers feel what the people on screen feel and buy the product.

Some ad campaigns have even played out like full TV series. The long face off between Pepsi and Coca Cola on the screen left few people cold. Fans waited for the next round and picked their side.

On the flip side, ads often bend reality to look their best, from how people look to how they live. The thin young models in ads for beauty products, clothes, or desserts do not speak to every viewer, and that gap can stir up disappointment and self image issues for some.

So, when you tell stories and spread your ideas, keep healthy human relationships in mind. Reach for people's feelings and experiences, not just the surface stuff. Let your audience be themselves and enjoy using your services.

chapters.title: In closing
chapters.text:
Modern businesses and marketing campaigns should chase a useful kind of virality. You can set a strong example in a few ways. Find a fresh way to reach your audience, the way Steve Jobs did. Understand the real wishes of your customers, the way social worker Koreen Johannessen does. Surprise your buyers, the way Blendtec's Tom Dickson or journalist Denise Grady does. Pull people in with a one of a kind experience, the way the owners of the Please Don't Tell bar do.

The recipe is not hidden. People will pay attention to your services and pass them along when the products make them feel better. If you can also place your brand's story inside the wider story of human life and earn social proof, you will hold the keys to virality.

A short exercise. Put your idea to the test and lay out a plan to spread it. How many people know about it today. How will it make their lives better. What feelings does your product bring, and what story does it tell. Why would people want to tell others about it.
